title: Spanish Patatas Bravas

subs.name: Cauliflower florets roasted or fried instead of potato for a lower-carb variation that absorbs the sauce beautifully
Two tablespoons of tomato paste with an added 3 tablespoons of water gives a richer, more concentrated brava sauce

ingredients.name: olive oil (or sunflower oil for frying)
smoked paprika or Kashmiri red chilli powder

subs.name: Kashmiri red chilli powder is the best Indian substitute for smoked paprika — similar mild heat and deep red colour; add a tiny pinch of cayenne if you want more heat
Fresh lemon juice in equal quantity; adds brightness in a slightly different direction but works well

steps.text: Cut the potatoes into rough 2 cm cubes — they don't need to be perfect. Pat them very dry with a kitchen towel or paper; any moisture on the surface will cause the oil to splatter and the potatoes to steam rather than crisp.

Heat the frying oil in a deep kadai to about 175°C — a cube of potato dropped in should sizzle briskly and rise to the surface within 10 seconds. Fry the potato cubes in two batches for 7–8 minutes each until golden and crisp all over. Drain on paper towels and season with salt immediately.

While the potatoes drain, make the brava sauce. Heat the 4 tablespoons of olive oil in a saucepan over medium flame. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant, then add the chopped tomatoes and the paprika.

Cook the sauce over medium heat for 10–12 minutes, stirring regularly, until the tomatoes break down completely and the oil begins to separate around the edges of the pan. Add the vinegar and a good pinch of salt, stir well, and taste — the sauce should be tangy, slightly smoky, and boldly seasoned.

For a smooth sauce, let it cool slightly and blend in a mixie or with an immersion blender until glossy and pourable. For a rustic version, leave it chunky. Pile the hot fried potatoes in a serving dish and spoon the brava sauce generously over the top — do not toss or the potatoes will lose their crunch. Serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: red-chilli-powder → Kashmiri red chilli powder is the best Indian substitute for smoked paprika — similar mild heat and deep red colour; add a tiny pinch of cayenne if you want more heat; vinegar → Fresh lemon juice in equal quantity; adds brightness in a slightly different direction but works well; tomato → Two tablespoons of tomato paste with an added 3 tablespoons of water gives a richer, more concentrated brava sauce; potato → Cauliflower florets roasted or fried instead of potato for a lower-carb variation that absorbs the sauce beautifully
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A deep kadai is ideal for frying the potato cubes — the high sides prevent splashing. Kashmiri red chilli powder is a near-perfect substitute for smoked paprika and is available everywhere. Any cooking oil (sunflower, groundnut) is fine for frying; the olive oil amount in the sauce is small enough to use the real thing. The brava sauce can be made ahead and refrigerated for up to three days — just fry the potatoes fresh when ready to serve, as they go soft quickly once dressed.
